coredata.dc:description: Photodynamic (PDT) is a new strategy for non-invasive tumor therapy and has been studied for the treatment of oral cancer. PDT has the advantages of low damage and high therapeutic efficiency. However, conventional photosensitizers have defects in different degrees, such as low stability and solubility, and low oxygen quantum yield of singlet oxygen. These defects greatly limit the efficiency of PDT. Several studies have shown that carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), are promising as a novel nanoscale photosensitizer to mediate PDT for cancer treatment. In this study, we utilized fluorescent CDs doped with nitrogen to optimize their photosensitizing activity to enhance PDT in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC). Nitrogen-doped CDs were able to generate a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under 660-nm laser irradiation after uptake by cancer cells, effectively inducing apoptosis in the mitochondrial pathway. The in vivo and in vivo experiments verified that CDs-mediated PDT was able to promote apoptosis of tumor cells in terms of DNA damage, inhibition of angiogenesis and cell proliferation, etc. CDs, as a novel photosensitizer, is expected to be an outstanding tool for clinical PDT in the treatment of OSCC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs: (A) TEM image; (B) TEM image; (C) UV-irradiated CDs solutions; (D) XRD pattern; (E) FT-IR spectra; (F) XPS spectra; (G) Zeta Potential; (H) Maximum PL excitation and emission spectra; (I) UV–vis absorption spectra; (J-L) High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s peak of CDs.

Biocompatibility studies and cellular uptake of CDs. (A) Cell viability of three kind of cells incubated with various concentrations of CDs; (B) CLSM images of CAL-27 cells after incubation with CDs for different times (Red: CDs: Ex: 580 nm, Em: 635 nm; Blue: DAPI: Ex: 405 nm, Em: 470 nm); (C) Mean CDs fluorescence intensity (FL) of CAL-27 cells in (B); (D) CLSM images of CAL-27 cells after incubation with CDs for different times with or without laser irradiation (Green: DCF: Ex: 488 nm, Em: 525 nm); (E) FL images of Calcein AM/PI co-staining CAL-27 cells (Green: Calcein AM: Ex: 488 nm, Em: 515 nm; Red: PI: Ex: 535 nm; Em: 615 nm); (F) Mean DCF FL fluorescence intensity of CAL-27 cells in (D). Data are expressed as means± SD. (n = 3).

Evaluation of PDT
in vitro.
(A) Cell viability of CAL-27 cells incubated with CDs under 660-nm laser irradiation; (B) CLSM images of Edu co-staining CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation (Blue: Hoechst 33342: Ex: 405 nm, Em: 461 nm; Red: EdU: Ex: 590 nm, Em: 615 nm); (C) Scratch width variation of CAL-27 cells incubated with CDs under 660-nm laser irradiation after 12 h (Green: AO: Ex: 488 nm, Em: 525 nm); (D) Images of CAL-27 cells incubated with CDs in transwell chambers under 660-nm laser irradiation after 12 h, 24 h; (E) Mean Edu FL fluorescence intensity of CAL-27 cells in (B); (F) Relative migration rate of CAL-27 cells in (C); (G) Number of cells penetrated to lower chamber in (D). Data are expressed as means± SD. (n = 3).

Pro-tumor cell apoptosis studies with CDs-mediated PDT. (A) Schematic representation of apoptosis in tumor cells after PDT; (B) CLSM images of JC-1 co-staining mitochondria in CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation (Green: JC-1 Monomers: Ex: 515 nm, Em: 525 nm; Red: JC-1 Aggregates: Ex: 585 nm, Em: 590 nm); (C) CLSM images of γ-H2AX in CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation (Green: γ-H2AX: Ex: 488 nm, Em: 515 nm; Blue: DAPI: Ex: 405 nm, Em: 470 nm); (D) Flow cytometry analysis of CAL-27 cells incubated with CDs under 660-nm laser irradiation by the FITC and PI channel; (E) Mean JC-1 FL fluorescence intensity of CAL-27 cells in (B); (F) Mean γ-H2AX FL fluorescence intensity of CAL-27 cells in (C); (G)Apoptosis rate of CAL-27 cells in (D). Data are expressed as means± SD. (n = 3).

Expression of apoptotic proteins in CAL-27 cells after PDT. (A) CLSM images of Bax in CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation; (B) CLSM images of Bcl-2 in CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation; (C) CLSM images of Caspase-3 in CAL-27 cells after 660-nm laser irradiation; (D) Mean Bax, Bcl-2, and Caspase-3 FL fluorescence intensity of CAL-27 cells in (A-C). (Red: Ex: 535 nm, Em: 617 nm; Blue: DAPI, Ex: 405 nm, Em: 470 nm).

Evaluation of PDT
in vivo
. (A) Schematic representation of CDs-mediated PDT for in vivo therapy. (B) Photographs of excised tumors at 20 d; (C and D) Growth curves of body weight and tumors volume on CAL-27 cells orthotopic tumor-bearing mice after various treatment; (E) Tumor growth inhibition after 20 days; (F) H&E staining of the tumor tissues at 30 d and immunofluorescence staining of relative biomarkers of cell apoptosis (CD31, Ki67 and TUNEL) of tumors in CAL-27 cells orthotopic tumor-bearing mice after various treatments. Data are expressed as means± SD. (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a highly efficient photosensitizer for photodynamic therapy to promote apoptosis in oral squamous cell carcinoma

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photodynamic (PDT) is a new strategy for non-invasive tumor therapy and has been studied for the treatment of oral cancer. PDT has the advantages of low damage and high therapeutic efficiency. However, conventional photosensitizers have defects in different degrees, such as low stability and solubility, and low oxygen quantum yield of singlet oxygen. These defects greatly limit the efficiency of PDT. Several studies have shown that carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), are promising as a novel nanoscale photosensitizer to mediate PDT for cancer treatment. In this study, we utilized fluorescent CDs doped with nitrogen to optimize their photosensitizing activity to enhance PDT in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC). Nitrogen-doped CDs were able to generate a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under 660-nm laser irradiation after uptake by cancer cells, effectively inducing apoptosis in the mitochondrial pathway. The in vivo and in vivo experiments verified that CDs-mediated PDT was able to promote apoptosis of tumor cells in terms of DNA damage, inhibition of angiogenesis and cell proliferation, etc. CDs, as a novel photosensitizer, is expected to be an outstanding tool for clinical PDT in the treatment of OSCC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots and carbon dot-mediated photodynamic therapy to promote tumor cell apoptosis

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the World Health Organization (WHO) 2019, cancer tops the list of causes of death in countries around the world, reflecting the aging of the population and changes in the level of socio-economic development [1]. Oral squamous cell carcinoma is the most common pathological type of oral malignancy, accounting for about 80–90 % of the cases, which greatly threatens human health and quality of life, and also creates a great healthcare burden for society. To date, surgical resection, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy remain the most prominent strategies for cancer treatment. However, these traditional treatments inevitably have many drawbacks and challenges, bringing serious side effects such as tissue defects and deformities, nausea, and various psychological disorders [2,3].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive local therapeutic strategy that has emerged in recent years and has become an alternative or auxiliary approach to tumor treatment due to its advantages of fewer side-effects, less likely to develop drug resistance, and less invasive [4–6]. The mechanism of PDT is that in the presence of photosensitizer, local irradiation of the tumor site can stimulate the interaction between the photosensitizer and the surrounding oxygen molecules to produce highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) substances to produce cytotoxicity thereby causing apoptosis, autophagy or necrosis of tumor cells [7–10]. Many studies have demonstrated the therapeutic effects of photodynamic therapy. He et al. designed and synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) using diketopyrrolopyrrole and chitosan as raw materials in a one-pot hydrothermal method, which was used in PDT of hepatocellular carcinoma cells (HepG2) under 540-nm laser irradiation, demonstrating that such CDs have the potential to be an effective cancer treatment [11]. Li et al. also synthesized sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) with a high yield of singlet oxygen as nano-photosensitizers to improve PDT efficacy and found that S-CDs-treated cells had a better level of apoptotic protein expression when compared with the conventional photosensitizer 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) [12].

Photosensitizers play an important role in PDT—after being excited by light of a specific wavelength and absorbing energy, they are transformed sequentially from the ground state into an excited unilinear state and a long-lived excited trilinear state, which reacts in the presence of oxygen to form ROS such as singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (O2 -) and hydroxyl radical -OH) [13]. Although several porphyrin-based photosensitizers have been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for clinical use, there is imperative for photosensitizers that meet more demanding requirements for the PDT of cancer: such as high singlet oxygen quantum yields, easy enrichment in tumor tissues, rapid metabolism from the body, and low dark cytotoxicity [14,15]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a photosensitizer with good biocompatibility, good bioimaging ability, and high photosensitivity activity.

Novel nanomaterials based on nanotechnology provide new ideas. CDs, a new type of fluorescent carbonaceous nanoparticles, have greatly attracted attention in the past decade or so due to their fascinating structures and unique optical properties. CDs have been used in biosensing, catalysis, bioimaging, and drug delivery with great success. More notably, CDs can respond to visible light irradiation due to their conjugated carbon structure and may be used as photosensitizers to generate ROS in PDT to achieve cancer therapeutic effects [11,16–18].

Therefore, we creatively used hyaluronic acid (HA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs with high photosensitivity activity, which have good water solubility, biocompatibility, and stability. Due to nitrogen doping, CDs possess excellent fluorescence properties and high ROS generation efficiency and quantum yield, which can achieve efficient killing of tumor cells after light irradiation at a lower concentration and promoting apoptosis in tumor cells by the mitochondrial pathway [19]. Meanwhile, the excellent biocompatibility and imaging capability of CDs have also expanded the application of CDs in the field of bioimaging.

HA and PEI were purchased from Merck Sigma-Aldrich Group; the rest of the reagents were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (China) Co. Human tongue squamous carcinoma cell line (CAL-27), mouse breast cancer cell line (4T1), and human oral mucosal keratinized epithelial cells (Hok) were obtained from the laboratory of West China College of Stomatology, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China, and 4-week-old BALB/c Nude mice were purchased from the Beijing Weitong Lihua Laboratory Animal Technology Co. The experiment followed the Code of Ethics for Animal Experiments of Shanxi Medical University. Ultrapure deionized water was used for all the experiment.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using HA as the carbon source and PEI as the nitrogen source and were processed at 180 °C for 10 h. Referring to previously reported literature, we added HA and PEI into ultrapure water separately and transferred the mixture to a Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined high-temperature reactor, which was processed at 180 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was complete, the reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature, and the obtained CDs solution was filtered through 0.22-μm microporous filter membranes and dialyzed by osmotic membranes (MWCO = 1 KD) for 48 h. Finally, the purified CDs solution was lyophilized in a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain CDs powder [20].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were determined by a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope, and the crystal structure of CDs was identified using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D2 PHASER, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded using an EQUINOX 55 instrument and an AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer was used to investigate the surface functional groups and elemental composition of the CDs. The surface potential of the materials was analyzed by a zeta potential analyzer (ZPA 20, Germany) to assess their stability. UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan), photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dark cytotoxicity and cell proliferation assays
ce:section.ce:para: The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay was used to detect the dark cytotoxicity of CDs and the effect of different laser power densities on cell proliferation. Firstly, CAL-27, 4T1 and HOK cells were inoculated in 96-well plates with 2×104 cells per well, respectively, and incubated in a constant temperature incubator overnight (37 ℃, 5 % CO2). Thereafter, the medium was replaced with 100 µL of DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (10, 100, 200, 500, 1000 µg/mL) and incubated again overnight (6 replicate wells were set for each group). Then 10 µL of CCK-8 medium was added to each well and incubation was continued for 2 h. The optical density (OD) at 450 nm was determined for each well using an enzyme marker. Similarly, CAL-27 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates and DMEM containing different concentrations of CDs (100, 200, 500, 1000 µg/mL) was added after overnight incubation. Subsequently, different 660 nm laser irradiation powers (10, 20, 50, 100, 200 mW/cm2) were set to irradiate for 10 min to explore the optimal parameters for cellular experiments with PDT. After irradiation, the cells were incubated for 4 h away from light, and the rest of the treatments were as above.

The effect of PDT on the viability of CAL-27 cells was examined using the Calcein-AM/PI Live/Dead Cell Double Staining Kit. CAL-27 cells were inoculated at a density of 1×105 cells/well in 24-well plates (sterile coverslips were placed in each well) and incubated overnight before the medium was changed to DMEM containing 500 µg/mL CDs. The plates were incubated again overnight and then irradiated with 660-nm (100 mW/cm2) laser for 10 min. After laser irradiation, cells were incubated for 4 h protected from light, then Calcein-AM/PI stained for 30 min and imaged by confocal laser microscopy (CLSM). Live cells appeared green (Calcein-AM), while dead cells turned into red (PI).

The uptake of CDs by CAL-27 cells was examined using CLSM. CAL-27 cells were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 1×105 cells/well (sterile coverslips were placed in each well) and incubated overnight. After replacing the old medium with DMEM containing 500 µg/mL CDs and incubating for 0.5, 1, 2 and 4 h, respectively, the cells were washed in PBS and fixed in 4 % formaldehyde. Cell nucleus were stained with 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and blocked. The final results were imaged using CLSM.

Intracellular ROS were detected using a probe, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), which is not fluorescent per se but is oxidized in the presence of ROS and emits green fluorescence.CAL-27 cells were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 1×105 cells/well (sterile coverslips were placed in each well) and incubated overnight. The old medium was replaced with DMEM containing 500 µg/mL CDs and incubated for 2 h. Fresh medium containing DCFH-DA (20 μm) was added after washing the cells three times with PBS and incubated for another 30 min in the dark. Results were obtained using CLSM after 10 min of 660-nm laser irradiation (100 mW/cm2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocytotoxicity and cellular growth capacity assays
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the therapeutic effect of CDs, cell viability was measured under different treatments. The treatments were consistent with the dark cytotoxicity assay, except that 660 nm laser irradiation for 10 min (100 mW/cm2) was required to continue the incubation for 12, 24, 48 and 72 h after cellular uptake of CDs. The effect of PDT on the proliferation of CAL-27 cells was assessed from the level of DNA synthesis using the EdU molecular probe. Cells were incubated with 500 µg/mL CDs overnight and then irradiated with 660-nm (100 mW/cm2) laser for 10 min. Cells were incubated out of light for 4 h and then incubated with EdU molecular probes out of light for 4 h. Cell nucleus were labeled with Hoechst 33342 and imaged with CLSM.

Scratch assay was performed to assess the migration ability of CAL-27 cells after PDT. CAL-27 cells were inoculated in 6-well plates at a density of 5×105 cells/well and incubated overnight, and a monolayer of cell scratches was made after the cells had grown all over the bottom of the plate. The incubation was continued for 12 hours by replacing the old medium with DMEM containing 500 µg/mL CDs and 660-nm laser irradiation for 10 minutes (100 mW/cm2). The wounds were then stained with acridine orange (AO), and the wound distances at 0 h and 12 h were recorded under an inverted fluorescence microscope and further analyzed for an area of wound healing.

CAL-27 cells were inoculated in transwell chambers with the bottom of the chambers (polycarbonate membrane) separating the upper layer of medium containing CDs (500 µg/mL) and the lower layer of medium without CDs. After incubation for 4 h, the experimental group was given 660-nm laser irradiation (100 mW/cm2 for 10 min), and the control group was not irradiated. After continuing the incubation for 24 h, the uninvaded cells were removed from the upper surface and the cells invading the lower surface were stained with crystal violet solution and subsequently photographed by microscope.

According to a previous study, JC-1 was used to study the changes in cellular MMP after PDT [21]. In general, in normal cells, JC-1 can selectively enter mitochondria as aggregates and emit red fluorescence. When MMP is lost, JC-1 dissipates into the cytoplasm and forms monomers that emit green fluorescence. After inoculation with CAL-27 cells, the cells were walled and incubated with medium containing CDs and finally treated with 660-nm laser (100 mW/cm2, 10 min). 4 h later, the cells were stained with JC-1 (10 mg/mL) for 10 min in the dark. The fluorescence intensity of cells in each group was observed by CLSM and photographed and recorded.

CAL-27 cells in the logarithmic growth phase were taken and inoculated on cell crawls in 24-well plates at a density of 1×106 cells/well, and cultured in a cell incubator for 24 h. Medium containing CDs (500 µg/mL) was added, and the culture continued for 24 h. After fixing the cells, the cells were permeabilized, closed, and incubated with monoclonal antibody to γ-H2AX and secondary antibody in turn, and then DAPI dye was added for 5 min, and then the slices were sealed by washing with PBS again 3 times, and the protein expression was finally observed by CLSM.

CAL-27 cells were inoculated in confocal Petri dishes incubated with medium with/without CDs after selective treatment with or without PDT (100 mW/cm2, 10 min). All cells were washed 3 times with PBS and then 4 % paraformaldehyde was added to fix the cells for 30 min. Cells were incubated in 0.1 % Triton X-100 for 15 min, washed with PBS, and then closed in 5 % goat serum for 2 h. The cells were then co-incubated with primary antibodies (Bax, Bcl-2, Caspase-3) at 4 °C overnight. Next, cells were incubated with secondary antibodies for 2 h. Cell nucleus were stained with DAPI. The cells were finally observed by CLSM.

Briefly, CAL-27 cells were first inoculated in 6-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Cultured with CDs for 12 h, CAL-27 cells were exposed to 660-nm laser for 10 min and cells were collected. Apoptosis was studied by flow cytometry analysis using the Annexin V-FITC/PI Apoptosis Detection Kit according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Female BALB/c nude mice (4–6 weeks old) were kept in a laboratory free of specific pathogens. CAL-27 cells (1×106) were resuspended in 100 μL of physiological saline and were injected subcutaneously into the right axilla of each mouse to generate tumors. When the tumor volume reached 100–120 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into 4 groups, which were divided into saline group (Blank), saline+laser (Blank+laser), CDs group, and CDs+laser group and the therapeutic dose of CDs was 10 mg/kg. Next, 24 h after the injection, the mice in each group were irradiated with 660-nm laser (250 mW/cm2, 30 min). During the treatment period, the mice body weight and tumor volume were measured every 2 days. After 20 days of treatment, all mice were euthanized and tumors and major organs were collected for histological analysis. Typically, tissues were fixed in 4 % formaldehyde, dehydrated in ethanol, and then embedded in paraffin. Subsequently, the in vivo biocompatibility of the materials was assessed by hemolysis, H&E staining and TUNEL staining were used to determine the therapeutic effect. Photographs of the sections were taken using a light microscope.

After the tumor tissue Section (4 μm) were deparaffinized and placed in citrate buffer for antigen extraction, the sample sections were incubated with primary antibodies Ki67 (1:100, Beyotime), and CD31 (1:100, CST) at 4 °C overnight. Antibody-conjugated tissues were stained using a DAB assay kit (Solarbio, China) for 5 min at room temperature and then restained with hematoxylin. The stained sections were observed under a light microscope and analyzed for protein expression levels.

SPSS 26.0 software was used for data analysis. All experiments were repeated at least three times to ensure the validity of observations, and all values are expressed as means ± standard deviations (SD). Statistical analysis was performed by t-test and one-way ANOVA results. In all results, P < 0.05 was considered significant for all comparisons.

CDs were prepared using a conventional one-step hydrothermal method using HA and PEI as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. Since both HA and PEI are polymers, their residues will be retained on the surface of CDs under relatively mild preparation conditions [22]. The abundant nitrogen in PEI could change the absorption and emission peak positions of CDs, thus improving the fluorescence properties and photosensitivity of CDs. HA, a major component of the extracellular matrix (ECM) composed of disaccharide repeating units, not only recognized the CD44 receptor, which is expressed at high levels on the surface of cancer cells, and conferred a certain degree of tumor-active targeting of CDs, but also improved the biocompatibility of its residues [20]. Such CDs could act as photosensitizers in direct response to irradiation, generating ROS through electron or energy transfer processes [23]. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) in Fig. 1A and 1B, the CDs were relatively uniform spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of 3.1 ± 0.6 nm. The obtained CDs had distinct lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of about 0.2 nm, which was in line with that of the graphite (100) surface. Fig. 1C showed the bright orange-red fluorescence of the CDs solution under UV light irradiation. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs was shown in Fig. 1D, which showed an obvious strong diffraction peak at 23.52°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon.

The surface functional groups of CDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) as shown in Fig. 1E. The absorption peak at 3345 cm−1 corresponded to a strong stretching vibration of the -OH peak, while the -NH2 peak at almost the same position was covered by the broad -OH peak. The absorption peaks at 2952 cm−1 and 740 cm−1 corresponded to the -CH3 and -CH bonds, respectively. The CC and CO bonds gave rise to peaks at 1695 cm−1 and 1554 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1440 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of the -C-N bond. The whole FT-IR spectra confirmed the presence of amino, hydroxyl, and unsaturated bonds on the surface of CDs, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further confirmed the presence of these group elements in CDs (Fig. 1F). Among them, the contents of C, N, and O were 68.65 %, 12.79 %, and 18.29 %, respectively, and their respective characteristic peaks were located at 284.8 eV, 399.43 eV, and 531.12 eV. The abundance of functional groups on the surfaces made the CDs easy to be surface-modified and functionalized, which broadened the possibilities of biological applications. Fig. 1J-L showed high-resolution XPS spectra of the elements of CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1J) showed the presence of three main types of C: C-C/ CC (284.3 eV), C-O/C-N (285.6 eV) and CO/C-N (287.4 eV) [24,25]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 1K) showed that the main peaks of N-H and C-N correspond to 399.2 eV and 400.2 eV, respectively [26]. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s in Fig. 1L showed two characteristic peaks observed at 530.8 eV and 531.7 eV, associated with the vibrations of the O-H and CO groups, respectively [27]. Wu et al. found that there existed a link between photo-oxidative activity and the nitrogen content of the CDs. They emphasized the important role of nitrogen doping in improving the photosensitive properties of CDs [28]. The zeta potential of the CDs in water was −7.07 mv (negative zeta potential), as shown in Fig. 1G, which indicated that the aqueous solution of CDs was relatively dispersed conducive to cellular uptake, suggesting the stability of the system.

The CDs also had good stability in other solvents, as shown in Fig. S1. After equal amounts of CDs were dispersed in double distilled water (ddH2O), DMEM culture medium, phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) placed at room temperature for 0, 14 and 21 days, the solution color of the whole system was uniform without precipitation (Fig.S1A-C). Meanwhile, the corresponding emission spectra showed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in each solution system remained almost unchanged after 21 days of placement. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) images also showed that the average particle size of the CDs did not change significantly, but the frequency of the distribution decreased slightly, which might be related to the fact that agglomerated CDs would gradually disperse, proving that their dispersion and particle size distribution were stable (Fig. S1D-G). The optical properties of CDs were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 1I, the absorption spectrum of CDs showed broad absorption at 300–550 nm. The absorption peak appearing at 349 nm corresponded to the π-π* leap absorption of the distribution of CC/CO functional groups, and the peak at 203 nm represented the π-π* leap induced by the aromatic structure of the sp2 domain. It can also be seen that the optimum emission wavelength of the CDs solution under 500 nm excitation was 583 nm (Fig. 1H), which could be attributed to the nitrogen doping, and also to the orange-red color of the CDs solution under UV light. We synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs with a QY of 21.8 %. Elemental doping altered the unique electrical structure and optical properties of CDs and increases the number of active sites on its surface, thus enhancing its fluorescence quality [29]. As shown in Fig.S2 is the time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy of nitrogen-doped CDs, the fluorescence lifetime value is 3.31 ns, which represents a better fluorescence stability. The complex multi-exponential decay pattern implied that the nitrogen-doped CDs have non-single emitters, consistent with carbon quantum dots with typical structural defects or surface states [30,31].
To sum up, these results indicated that the CDs with high nitrogen content, stability, solubility as well as good fluorescence properties and can be used for biomedical applications.

The dark cytotoxicity of CDs as well as the efficiency of PDT was explored before conducting in vitro experiments on PDT. CDs have been shown to be low cytotoxicity/highly biocompatible nanoparticles for normal cells [32,33]. In this study, the experiments were mainly selected CAL-27 cells for a series of manipulations. In order to explore the biosafety of CDs, CAL-27, 4T1 and Hok cells were selected to be tested by CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 2A, different concentrations (10–1000 µg/mL) of CDs were cytotoxic to the two cancer cells, whereas the normal cell Hok maintained more than 90 % cell viability. Therefore, the CDs have almost negligible cytotoxicity to normal cells but have certain killing ability to cancer cells. Meanwhile, different parameters were set to investigate the study of cancer cells killing by different laser power densities. As shown in Fig. S3, exposure to the 660-nm laser for 10 min resulted in a killing efficiency of 500 mW/cm2 that was similar to that of 1000 mW/cm2 but with a lower energy consumption, thus determining that the optimal power density to use for this PDT was 500 mW/cm2. Therefore, such nitrogen-doped CDs improve the therapeutic efficiency of PDT while avoiding excessive energy loss, effectively killing cancer cells while protecting normal cells from damage.

Intracellular uptake of CDs by CAL-27 cells was explored using CLSM. The cell nucleus was stained with DAPI to clarify whether CDs would localize there, as the efficacy of PDT is largely dependent on the intracellular localization of photosensitizers [34]. CLSM images (Fig. 2B) showed that CDs (red fluorescence) were largely distributed in the cytoplasm rather than in the nucleus. In addition, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity could be seen during incubation for 0.5–2 h, with a slight decrease at 4 h. The results indicated that CDs were most highly chemotaxis at 2 h and had a time-dependent effect, similar to the findings of Wen et al. [35]. In addition, the bright red fluorescence indicated that CDs could be a reliable fluorescent probe for bioimaging. PDT generates large amounts of ROS capable of interacting with biomolecules leading to direct or indirect damage [36]. CDs receive light and absorb energy from the ground state to an excited state, and electron transfer occurs between their excited state and intracellular substrates (e.g. nucleic acids, proteins and lipids) to form free radicals, which interact with O2 to generate hydrogen peroxide, superoxide anion, and hydroxyl radicals (H2O2, O2 -, and -OH), i.e., type I reaction [37]. There is also a direct transfer of energy from excited-state CDs to oxygen molecules to produce 1O2, i.e., type II reaction [38]. These two reactions can occur simultaneously, producing H2O2, O2 -, -OH and 1O2, collectively known as ROS [23,39,40]. Before performing intracellular PDT, the ability of CDs to generate ROS within the cell needed to be examined. DCFH-DA, which is not fluorescent by itself, was chosen as an indicator of intracellular ROS production, but it rapidly oxidized in the presence of ROS to form green fluorescent dichlorofluorescein (DCF). As shown in Fig. 2D, bright green fluorescence was observed in CAL-27 cells after 10 min of exposure to 660-nm laser. With the increase of time (0–4 h), the intracellular DCF production increased, effectively proving a large amount of ROS production. On the contrary, in the CDs group, no green fluorescence production was observed before 2 h, and there was a weak green fluorescence from 2 to 4 h, suggesting that CDs could produce a small amount of ROS without laser excitation. It can be hypothesized that nitrogen-doped CDs-mediated PDT action led to the generation of excessive ROS inside the cell, bursting and flooding the cell's internal organelles, resulting in decreased cell viability and eventual death. In order to further investigate and determine the ROS components of nitrogen-doped CDs that played a major role in the PDT process, we explored them with several fluorescent probes. The 1O2 produced by nitrogen-doped CDs was detected using a Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green Fluorescent Probe (SOSG). SOSG is a probe that binds 1O2 highly selectively without any significant response to -OH, O2 - and nitric oxide (NO). The presence of internal electron transfer before the reaction of SOSG with 1O2 leads to quenching of the fluorescence. When SOSG reacts with 1O2 to form SOSG endoperoxide (SOSG-EP), the internal electron transfer is prevented and green fluorescence is observed [41]. Fig. S4 showed the change of fluorescence intensity of SOSG at the optimal emission wavelength (530 nm) with the different time of laser irradiation. For SOSG with the laser irradiation group alone, the fluorescence intensity was constant. The increase in fluorescence intensity was significantly lower than in the CDs group compared with CDs+laser irradiated group. This suggested that PDT can increase the efficiency of 1O2 production by CDs. Dihydroethidium (DHE) is used to specifically indicate the formation of O2 -. After uptake by living cells, DHE is dehydrogenated in the presence of intracellular O2 to produce ethidium, which can bind to RNA or DNA to produce red fluorescence. In contrast, dihydroergotamine 123 (DHR 123), while not directly indicative of the presence of O2- or H2O2, can oxidize DHR 123 to rhodamine 123, which exhibits green fluorescence when these ROS are combined with other cellular components such as cytochrome c oxidase or Fe2+ [42–44]. Fig. S5 displayed that the CDs+laser group showed bright red fluorescence after 660-nm laser irradiation compared to the rest of the groups. And green fluorescence appeared in each group under the same treatment conditions. However, the intensity was not high, indicating that the oxidation degree of DHR123 did not differ much between the groups and that cells also have ROS intermediates in the unstimulated state. In conclusion, nitrogen-doped CDs could generate various types of ROS, including 1O2, O2 - and H2O2, during PDT. Combined with the mechanism of CDs mediating the generation of ROS by PDT, it can be speculated that both type I and type II reactions occur during CDs receiving light source to produce enhanced PDT effects.

We also used the fluorescent probes Calcein-AM and PI for the identification of live cells (green) and dead cells (red), respectively to confirm the efficiency of this power density. The unirradiated CDs group had some killing ability on CAL-27 cells, but the percentage of dead cells was relatively small, whereas large areas of PI fluorescent cells were found in CAL-27 cells after 10 min of exposure to the 660 nm laser (Fig. 2E). This result suggested that laser irradiation after CDs treatment led to significant cancer cells death. The above results provided direct evidence of the cytotoxicity of CDs to cancer cell and the ability of intracellular ROS production under 660-nm laser irradiation, confirming that CDs could be taken up by cells as an efficient photosensitizer for PDT applications.

In summary, this kind of nitrogen-doped CDs had good biocompatibility with the ability to kill tumor cells, and the almost negligible toxicity to normal cells suggested that it could be used in biological experiments. Meanwhile, we noted that biological cells had good uptake ability for nitrogen-doped CDs, which could realize intracellular bright imaging, which was potentially important for dynamic monitoring and bioimaging.

To assess the therapeutic effect of PDT, cell viability, cell proliferation, and cell migration were studied separately. The photocytotoxicity of CDs was determined using the standard CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 3A, after 10 min of 660-nm laser irradiation (500 mW/cm2), CDs exhibited significant photocytotoxicity, and cell viability decreased to 30 %. While the unirradiated CDs group also showed some ability to inhibit cell viability, the cell viability of the remaining control group was almost unaffected. Therefore, CDs had good potential for PDT and could be used for further studies. Detection of DNA synthesis using EdU thus reflecting the proliferation of cancer cells. EdU is a non-radioactive analogue of thymine that can be incorporated into the DNA of proliferating cells [45]. As shown in Fig. 3B, bright red fluorescence was emitted from the cells, indicating that there was DNA assembly and synthesis in the cells, while the red fluorescent cells receiving laser irradiation were significantly reduced after CDs uptake. The results indicated that the number of cells assembling and synthesizing DNA was reduced in the CDs+laser group, and the proliferation ability was significantly inhibited.

Tumor cells have the ability to invade, and in order to test the effect of PDT on the mobile and invasive ability of CAL-27 cells, scratches were fabricated on a monolayer of CAL-27 cells. As shown in Fig. 3C, after creating equal-width scratches different treatments were applied to each group of cells, and incubation was continued for 12 h. The results showed that the width of the scratches was almost unchanged after 12 h of incubation in the CDs+laser group, while it was significantly narrowed in the blank group. Changes in the invasive ability of CDs on CAL-27 cells after laser irradiation were examined using transwell chambers. The membrane pores of the transwell chambers were covered by a filter membrane that simulated the extracellular matrix, and the tumor cells needed to secrete hydrolytic enzymes and go through deformation motions to penetrate to the lower chamber. As shown in Fig. 3D, CAL-27 cells ingested CDs and then irradiated with 660-nm laser for 10 min, and the migration of cells to the lower chamber could be observed after incubation for 12 and 24 h. The number of CAL-27 cells migrating to the lower chamber in the CDs+laser group was significantly less than that of the rest of the control group, which indicated that PDT was able to inhibit the secretion of hydrolytic enzymes by CAL-27 cells and their penetration through the filter membrane into the lower chamber. The above results provided preliminary evidence that CDs could affect the proliferation and mobility of CAL-27 cells after laser irradiation, which may be part of the efficacy of PDT.

It has been demonstrated that ROS can interact with biomolecules to produce damage and perhaps induce apoptosis [12]. To further investigate the role of PDT efficacy in causing tumor cell death, the more subtle intracellular responses to CDs-mediated PDT are discussed. Mitochondria are essential for cellular energy metabolism, apoptosis regulation, and signaling, and ROS-induced oxidative damage destroys mitochondria and nuclear DNA, causing an intracellular “imbalance” that leads to apoptosis ( Fig. 4A).

Generally, in normal cells, JC-1 can selectively enter mitochondria as aggregates and emit heavy orange fluorescence. When MMP is lost, JC-1 dissipates into the cytoplasm and forms green-fluorescent monomers [46]. As shown in Fig. 4B, both Blank and Blank+laser groups showed intense red fluorescence and a few aggregated green fluorescence, indicating little change in MMP. Interestingly, the bright green fluorescence shown in the CDs+laser group indicated a significant change in MMP, meaning that the ROS produced by PDT depolarized the mitochondrial membrane and altered its structure. And DNA was inevitably affected, as shown in Fig. 4C. One of the most recognized processes in the DNA damage response is the phosphorylation of the SQ motif in the H2AX histone, making γ-H2AX a marker of double-stranded DNA damage [47,48]. The number of γ-H2AX foci in the nucleus was significantly higher (green fluorescence) in the CDs+laser group compared to other groups, indicating double-stranded DNA damage in CAL-27 cells.

In addition, it has been shown that PDT-generated ROS induces apoptosis via the mitochondrial apoptotic pathway, causing changes in Bcl-2 family proteins (e.g., Bcl-2 and Bax), which in turn activate caspases [49,50]. To further investigate the therapeutic efficiency of CDs-mediated PDT, immunofluorescence analysis at the biomolecular level was used to study the changes in Bcl-2, Bax, and caspase-3 (apoptosis-related proteins). As shown in Fig. 5B, CLSM images showed that the fluorescence intensity of Bcl-2 in the CDs+laser group was the weakest among the above three proteins, while the opposite trend was observed for Bax and caspase-3. Bax and caspase-3 were only weakly expressed in the rest of the control group, while the signal intensity of Bcl-2 was more pronounced (Fig. 5A, C).

The apoptosis detection by flow cytometry also further confirmed the above conclusion (Fig. 4D). Flow cytometry analysis using Annexin V-FITC and PI Apoptosis Detection kit showed that the percentage of apoptotic cells in the CDs+laser group reached 55.60 %, whereas apoptotic cells in both Blank and Blank+laser groups did not exceed 3 %. In addition, nearly 36 % of apoptotic cells were also found in the CDs group, suggesting that CDs itself can also cause CAL-27 cell apoptosis, but its mediated PDT is more efficient. The above results further confirmed that CDs as a novel photosensitizer can effectively mediate PDT for promoting apoptosis in tumor cells.

Generally speaking, this part of the results supported our hypothesis that CDs can activate the apoptotic process in the mitochondrial pathway and damage the DNA of genetic material in cancer cells to enhance the therapeutic effect of PDT.

Encouraged by these results, we evaluated the in vivo therapeutic effects of CDs-mediated PDT in CAL-27 tumor-bearing mice. Before formal experiments, we designed a hemolysis assay for CDs to test their safety in vivo, and in general, a hemolysis rate of less than 5 % was considered safe. As shown in Fig. S6, the hemolysis rate of the CDs group with the highest concentration (1000 µg/mL) was also less than 5 %, indicating that CDs should be safe and nontoxic in the blood circulation of mice.

As shown in Fig. 6A, the tumor-bearing mice were treated for 20 days. It is noteworthy that the Blank group showed a slight decrease in body weight while the tumor volume increased significantly during the treatment period due to the lack of effective treatment, indicating a serious burden on the survival of the mice (Fig. 6B, C, D). The CDs group also exhibited some tumor inhibition, with a tumor suppression rate of around 60 %, while the tumor suppression rate in the CDs+laser group increased significantly after the addition of 660-nm laser irradiation. However, we suspected that the 660-nm laser produced a certain thermal effect, resulting in a certain degree of therapeutic effect on the tumors in the laser group as well (Fig. 6E).

On day 20 of treatment, mice were executed to collect tumors and major organs for further H&E staining and immunohistochemical (IHC) analysis. As shown in Fig. 6F and Fig. S8, H&E staining of the tumor tissues showed extensive cell necrosis and the presence of cellular gaps in the CDs+laser group, which perhaps facilitated the penetration of CDs into the deeper tissues. CD31 was mainly used to demonstrate the presence of endothelial cells and was particularly used to assess tumor angiogenesis [51]. There were more ring-like small blood vessel formations in the Blank and Blank+laser groups whereas angiogenesis was significantly reduced in the CDs and CDs+laser groups, suggesting that nutrient delivery and growth of tumor tissues were impeded. Ki67 is a cell-cycle-associated nuclear protein, and the more of its expression, the more cells are in the growth phase [52]. The results of Ki67 showed that tumor cell growth and division were affected by CDs-mediated PDT. The results of Ki67 showed that the growth and division of tumor cells received great influence from CDs-mediated PDT. To investigate whether apoptosis occurs in tumor tissues, DNA fragments that have undergone breakage can be detected using TUNEL assay [53]. The IHC results of the TUNEL assay showed a large area of bright green fluorescence, indicating that a large number of apoptotic cells were generated in the CDs+laser group after receiving laser irradiation. All the above results showed that CDs-mediated PDT was highly efficient. Meanwhile, H&E staining of major organs showed that none of the groups caused significant organ damage (Fig. S7), which, combined with the hemolysis tests, once again demonstrated the excellent biocompatibility and safety of CDs. Combined with these results, CDs have significant therapeutic efficacy without remarkable systemic cytotoxicity and can be a reliable candidate for cancer treatment.

Overall, our study designed a novel nanoscale photosensitizer with excellent biocompatibility to inhibit tumor invasion, providing a powerful tool for oral cancer therapy. This work provides a dual-modality therapeutic diagnostic strategy with excellent biocompatibility to inhibit tumor metastasis or recurrence; we anticipate that the design opportunities offered by this smart nanomedicine will surely play an important role in future therapeutic diagnostics.

Currently, the CDs used in our study have not undergone any active targeting modifications and lack the ability to actively localize tumor cells, which is one of the limitations of this study. Therefore, we plan to use targeted peptide modification of carbon nanocomposites with high photosensitivity activity and anticancer ability in our subsequent work and combine them with currently effective chemotherapeutic drugs to achieve the combined therapy of PDT with chemotherapy or even photothermal therapy, to achieve good therapeutic effects on malignant tumors and solve the current problems.

In conclusion, for the photodynamic promotion of OSCC apoptosis, we have prepared fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs with high photosensitivity activity. In this nanoscale photosensitizer, a large amount of elemental nitrogen was doped, which greatly improved the photosensitivity activity and fluorescence properties of CDs. CDs-mediated PDT induced apoptosis of tumor cells under 660-nm irradiation, which resulted in therapeutic effects and inhibited the metastasis and growth of tumor cells.
